title: Probability Distribution Analysis of the Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver
authors: Yi-Hua Lai, John P. T. Stenger, Gloria Bazargan, Igor V. Schweigert, Daniel Gunlycke
abstract: The cascaded variational quantum eigensolver (CVQE) circumvents the need for iterative communication between the quantum and classical processing units that is necessary in the conventional VQE algorithm. While CVQE offers complete freedom to choose the guiding state as input, not all guiding states suffice for solution accuracy, as well as resource efficiency. Our work presents a process based on trapezoidal-state preparation for selecting guiding states that yield accurate many-electron ground-state solutions with minimal resource consumption. By analyzing the state probability distributions at different stages of the CVQE calculations, we determine the optimal guiding-state parameters for given resource constraints. We demonstrate the process by comparing electronic energies along the minimal-energy path for a prototypical bimolecular reaction, $\mathrm{H}_2 + \mathrm{H}_2^+ \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_3^+ + \mathrm{H}$, using Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) computing.
summary: This paper investigates the Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver (CVQE), an algorithm designed to reduce communication between quantum and classical units. By analyzing probability distributions, the authors show how to optimize guiding-state parameters to maintain chemical accuracy and maximize signal-to-noise ratios on noisy quantum hardware.
main_problem: Optimizing the selection of guiding states in the Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver (CVQE) to achieve accurate many-electron ground-state solutions with minimal resource consumption and robustness against hardware noise.
main_result: The study demonstrates that CVQE can achieve chemical accuracy even with single-step adiabatic preparation and that tuning the adiabatic energy scale (h-Omega) can maximize the signal-to-noise ratio on real NISQ hardware.
method: The authors use a trapezoidal-state preparation method for the CVQE algorithm, comparing noiseless simulations with IBM quantum hardware (ibm_aachen) and utilizing classical benchmarks like CCSD and FCI.
model_or_system: A prototypical bimolecular reaction (H2 + H2+ -> H3+ + H) modeled using a many-electron Hamiltonian and a 256-dimensional Fock space, mapped to 8 qubits via the Jordan-Wigner transformation.
key_observables: Ground-state energies (Eg), reaction energies (Delta E), and probability distributions of the quantum state at various algorithm stages.
important_parameters: Discretization steps (K), adiabatic energy scale (h-Omega), sampling scale (shot counts), and the adiabaticity condition (h-Omega K^-1 << h-omega_0 << h-Omega).
main_assumptions: The use of a minimal STO-6G basis set and the assumption that the classical optimization can compensate for distortions in the guiding state if the sampled Fock state set is representative.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows energy evolution via discretization; Figure 2 plots the minimal-energy path; Figure 3 shows molecular structures; Figure 8 shows noiseless probability distributions; Figure 9 and 10 illustrate how the guiding-state distribution and sampling counts change with the adiabatic energy scale on real hardware.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CVQE algorithm, defines the trapezoidal-state preparation method, analyzes different parameter regimes (adiabaticity and sampling) on simulators, evaluates performance on real IBM hardware, and provides a symmetry-based justification for the Fock state distributions in the appendix.
why_interesting: It provides practical insights into error mitigation and parameter tuning for VQE-like algorithms on NISQ devices, specifically showing how to balance adiabaticity and noise susceptibility.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A schematic diagram of the energy E(t) of the state evolving from the model ground state |Φ0⟩to the trapezoidal state ˆUK,Ω|Φ0⟩as a function of the discretized parameter ηk with K = 5 steps of size ∆η = 1/K.

FIG. 2. Electronic energies along the minimal-energy path for reaction (1) computed by the NEB method using CCSD/def2- QZVP. Energies corresponding to the reactant, collision, and product complexes are highlighted in red at coordinate 0, 31, and 62, respectively.

Figure 4 presents the ground-state energies obtained by the CVQE method using Qiskit [38]’s noiseless simu- lator as the blue solid curve, which is close to the orange dashed curve obtained by classical FCI. Both sets of CVQE and FCI calculations use the STO-6G basis set and the correction Ebsc in Eq. (12). For comparison, HF energies with the larger basis set def2-QZVP are shown as the green dashed curve. Both the CVQE and FCI calculations yield the same reaction energy, ∆E = −1.804 eV.

FIG. 3. Representative molecular structures along the minimal-energy path for reaction (1). Hydrogen atoms are shown in blue and interatomic distances are shown in green and orange. Shown are the geometries of the (a) reactants — H2 + and H2 are separated, (b) well — the system is at the lowest energy during the reaction, and (c) products — H3 + and H are separated.

FIG. 4. Electronic energies along the minimum-energy path for reaction (1) with the reactant, well, and product states at coordinate 0, 31, and 62, respectively. The reactant-product energy differences are ∆E = −1.804 eV for CVQE and FCI, and ∆E = −1.659 eV for HF/def2-QZVP.
